user_query:
We're evaluating an untrusted skill before installation. Please do a pre-install security review of the entire directory under input/skill_to_review/ and give me three deliverables plus shared-brain entries:

1) Write a severity-ranked scan report to output/scan_report.md. Requirements for this report:
- Begin with an overall findings line that states counts in this exact format: "Findings: <critical> critical, <high> high, <medium> medium, <low> low" using integers for each bucket.
- Include a Findings section where each item lists: severity, a one-line description, the file path with a line number, the pattern name (use the canonical pattern name when applicable), and a short match snippet. Use one bullet per finding.
- Include a Recommendations section with concrete remediation steps grouped as bullets. Make them actionable and tied to the categories of issues you found.

2) Create a safe-to-share JSON version of the report using a local-only sanitization approach and save it to output/sanitized_report.json. The JSON must contain exactly these fields: sanitized_text (string), findings_summary (array of objects with {type, count}), and review_notes (array of strings). Do not include any raw secrets or tokens in sanitized_text.

3) Read input/evvo-strategy.md and write a go/no-go recommendation for using or integrating this skill into our stack using five strategy lenses. Save this to output/strategy_recommendation.md with the following five sections in order: Recommendation (a direct call), Framework applied (which lens and why), Evvo context (how this fits the reference), Risk/watch-out (one key risk), and Next action (one concrete next step).

4) Prepare two shared-brain queue entries (do not actually append anywhere). Save them verbatim, one-per-line, into output/shared_brain_queue_entries.md using this exact fact format: [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC] [SECTION] [agent-name] key = value. Create one entry in [SECURITY] summarizing the top risk you found for this skill (e.g., auto-exec via pipe-to-shell or dangerous calls) and one in [DECISIONS] capturing the go/no-go decision and rationale in a short key=value.

Be thorough in the scan: look for hidden instruction injections in comments, shell pipe-to-exec patterns, automatic/persistent triggers, encoding/obfuscation, instruction overrides, dangerous calls (e.g., eval/exec, os.system, subprocess with shell=true, pickle loads, yaml.load without SafeLoader), exfiltration cues, and supply-chain risks. Use consistent pattern names when you can so that the report is auditable.

Read only from input/ and write only to output/.
